data.title: VC Commentary on Epstein's AI Agent Devouring Software and Musk's Space Ambitions
data.description:
Source: All-In Podcast; Compiled & Organized by: Yuliya, PANews

All-In Podcast is one of the world's most popular technology and business podcasts, hosted by four top venture capitalists and friends. The four hosts are: Jason Calacanis (early investor in Uber and Robinhood, podcast host, responsible for managing the show), Chamath Palihapitiya (billionaire, founder of Social Capital, known as the "King of SPACs," with sharp insights), David Friedberg (founder of The Production Board, with a strong scientific background, known as the "Sultan of Science"), and David Sacks (America's first "AI and Crypto Tsar," a close friend of Elon Musk, co-founder of Craft Ventures, former PayPal executive, and recently deeply involved in US political activities). This week, Chamath is absent, and his "fifth best friend," Brad Gerstner, founder of Altimeter Capital, will fill in.

The four discussed recent hot topics, including the connection between Silicon Valley and espionage triggered by the Epstein Papers leak, the impact of AI on traditional software under the "SaaS is dead" argument, concerns about the risk of AI autonomy raised by the Moltbook forum, Musk's SpaceX and xAI merger plan, and the policy impact of Trump's nomination of Kevin Warsh as the new chairman of the Federal Reserve. The following is a detailed transcript of the conversation, translated by PANews: Silicon Valley Network King? Started paying attention to Bitcoin in 2011 (Background: On January 30, the U.S. Department of Justice released a large number of documents under the Epstein Papers Transparency Act, mentioning hundreds of tech executives and public figures, including host Jason Calacanis.) David Friedberg (Friedberg): Okay, JCal, your name appeared in the documents, and there were several emails from you. Prosecutor Friedberg has a few questions for you. Jason Calacanis (JCal): Okay, please go ahead. Friedberg: When did you first meet Jeffrey Epstein? JCal: I met him at a TED conference in the late 90s, specifically at a "Billionaire Dinner" hosted by my bookseller, John Brockman. Friedberg: Have you seen him in New York since then? Have you been to his home, office, or anywhere else in New York? JCal: I've probably spoken to him for a total of 45 minutes in my life. 30 of those minutes were in the late 90s when I was working for *Silicon Valley Journalist* magazine. He was a billionaire financier who wanted to invest in my magazine. I met with him for 30 minutes, but he thought my project was too small and not worth his involvement. Friedberg: Where did you meet? JCal: At his legendary townhouse. Friedberg: You've been to that house? JCal: Yes, I visited him once. After that, I've seen him about six more times at the TED Billionaires Dinner. Friedberg: You've never been to that "island"? JCal: Never, and I've never been invited to the "island," his plane, or his ranch—none of that. Friedberg: When you went to his house, did you see any young girls? Or did you see anything like what's described in the reports? JCal: No. Friedberg: Did anyone give you a massage? JCal: No. I did exchange an email with him, but I've forgotten it. In 2011, he emailed me asking if I could introduce him to the people talking about Bitcoin on my podcast at the time. I said, "Sure, no problem, I'll introduce you." I do thousands of these introductions every year, connecting our portfolio companies, startups, and billionaires—it's part of an early-stage investor's job. Friedberg: Didn't you realize at the time that he was a sexual harasser or something? JCal: Not at all. I think these things really came to light in 2018 when the Miami Herald published a detailed report exposing his heinous acts, and that's when I started to realize it. I've been calling for this: release all the Epstein Papers. What he did was appalling, and everyone involved must be brought to justice. That's it.

Friedberg: What about Ghislaine Maxwell? There's also a separate email between you and her in the papers.

JCal: I also met her at TED and in New York social circles.

When I met her, her father, Robert Maxwell, was apparently the owner of the *New York Daily News*, she herself was a media executive, and her sisters were involved in angel investing in tech startups. So they were part of that circle. Looking back, I was known early in my career as a “connector,” and *The New Yorker* even wrote a long article about me saying I knew everyone and connected everyone. I think Epstein or Gislan's interest in me might have been in my ability to connect them with high-profile individuals to serve their business interests. Friedberg: So you knew nothing about Epstein or Gislan's illegal activities and never participated in any of them? JCal: Absolutely not, I did not participate in any illegal activities. David Sacks (Sacks): Let me add a few observations. First, I trust JCal 100%. As I joked on the talk show, he wasn't exactly a major player in the big picture. We learned from the Epstein Papers that Epstein was a master of connections, and you were a connector; the probability of you two meeting at that time was practically 100%. But your overlap was very limited. Secondly, what I find interesting is that he was curious about the "Bitcoin guys" back in 2011, which in itself shows that, whatever Epstein did, he definitely had an early sense of positioning himself at the center of everything. JCal even warned him at the time that those were "crazy Bitcoin guys," crypto libertarians, not suited for business. JCal: That's an interesting point. I invited these people onto my podcast because when I first heard about Bitcoin, it was less than a dollar. I thought these people were a bit odd; they didn't seem like entrepreneurs looking to raise funds, more like a foundation, like Wikipedia—you couldn't invest in them. I did give him that warning. Sacks: Yes, but he wasn't intimidated by your words and became deeply involved later. There was a company called Blockstream, which he co-invested in with Reid Hoffman and Joi Ito, and some of the Bitcoin Core developers were in it. Now all of this is coming to light. Another interesting point is how the media reported on this. The New York Times had an article about Epstein's connections to Silicon Valley, and although your connection was very weak and peripheral, your photo was prominently featured. Meanwhile, those with deeper and more significant connections to Epstein were completely ignored. Why? Why were they targeting me and not Reid? Because you were labeled "right-wing" because of your connections to Elon Musk and this podcast. Look at that article. It's not just you; they're also fiercely attacking Peter Thiel and Elon Musk, but Reid Hoffman is completely overlooked. He's only mentioned in one sentence along with a few others. Bill Gates is the same in that article. But if you're talking about who had the longest and closest contact with Epstein, it's undoubtedly Gates and Reid. They were still in contact with him until his death (2018-2019), visiting his island, flying on his plane, and going to his ranch. Reid Hoffman was the one who introduced Epstein to Peter Thiel, Musk, and Zuckerberg, and organized that famous dinner. How can you not report this as the root of Epstein's involvement in Silicon Valley? Brad: That's insane. The New York Times clearly has a list of targets they approve of, people like Elon Musk and Peter Thiel who are "right-wing coded," and even J.Cal became a target because of our relationship. But those who donated hundreds of millions of dollars to the Democratic Party and funded dirty tricks against Trump have largely escaped unscathed. Frankly, this is a microcosm of systemic corruption and a crisis of trust in the nation. They are part of that cabal, part of a system that people are losing faith in. Friedberg: This guy is scum. But David, this is why nobody trusts institutions, powerful elites, or any of this garbage. This took years to leak out, and people want to move on, but they can't. Nobody here has been prosecuted. What about the people in the emails? Why don't we see any charges? This guy "committed suicide" in a heavily monitored cell, but there's no investigation into his cause of death. This completely destroys people's trust in the system. JCal: Why weren't the approximately 30 people investigated with him prosecuted? This is insane, it's like a bizarre conspiracy, and his death is clearly highly suspicious. Friedberg: Do you think it's because they couldn't find evidence of underage prostitution or sex trafficking? JCal: What was ultimately revealed was that his "non-prosecution agreement" with Miami included provisions that prevented all other involved parties from being prosecuted. So there's definitely something fishy going on. What role did the FBI play in this? They did a lot of investigation, why didn't they prosecute the others? It's very strange. Friedberg: This question reveals that a large number of very private communications of public figures have been made public. There's a great book called *The Light of Yesterday*, which discusses what would happen if everyone in the world had access to each other's information. Does this mean that wealthy and powerful people feel they have the right to act maliciously in private, and that this is simply their privilege? Brad: David, that's a downplaying of their scumbag behavior. The truth is, normal people don't do that, and we can't normalize it. What's worse, those who behaved the worst were sanctimoniously lecturing others throughout the entire period. When my sisters or mothers in rural Indiana hear these coastal elites lecturing them all day, and then compare that to what they read in the Epstein Papers, that's why we lack trust. $300 Billion Evaporated Overnight: AI Agents Devour SaaS Profits? (Background: Anthropic released a new feature for its AI assistant Claude, capable of handling draft laws and research, triggering a panic sell-off in the legal tech and SaaS industry as a whole, causing related companies' stock prices to plummet.) JCal: SaaS companies are collapsing. On February 3, software and data stocks wiped out $300 billion in market capitalization from the S&P 500. This was dubbed the "Claude Crash." The event severely impacted many legal tech companies, with Thomson Reuters falling 20%, Lexus Nexus down 15%, and LegalZoom down 15%. Meanwhile, the entire SaaS industry was also hit by the notion that "software will be replaced by customized tools," with Figma down 13%, Salesforce and ServiceNow both down 11%, and Adobe down 8%. Brad, in your opinion, what exactly happened? Brad: The numbers you're reporting are severely underestimated. We've already lost trillions of dollars in market capitalization. Figma is down 80% from its peak. It's a real disaster. I said in an interview with CNBC earlier this year that these stocks were all falling, and 90% of them deserved to fall. Let's look at some charts. SaaS companies' forward revenue multiples have fallen to an all-time low of 3.9x. Their free cash flow multiples are also at an all-time low. So, the software industry is undervalued not only in terms of revenue but also in terms of free cash flow, even though they are highly profitable businesses. Why is this? Their stock prices are falling not because of declining revenue—in fact, software companies' revenue growth is stable or even increasing. They're falling because we're discounting future uncertainty. When profound technologies like AI emerge, they make you question the certainty and sustainability of future free cash flows. Take Salesforce, for example; its free cash flow multiple dropped from 30x to 15x. This means that today's buyers believe they can only rely on cash flow for the next 15 years, whereas previously they were willing to pay for the next 30 years. Good heavens, with today's AI, we don't even know what will happen in seven years. This is why these companies are meeting earnings targets, but their stocks are falling. Sacks: I think people are exaggerating a bit when they say AI will destroy SaaS. Take a SaaS product like Salesforce, for example; it's a massive system that handles all customer contacts and revenue. You wouldn't want to replace it with poorly validated code that just came out of a code-assistant yesterday. Think about how many bugs Salesforce's codebase has fixed over the past 25 years—probably millions. The system has been proven with thousands of large enterprise customers. However, there are some issues. If your SaaS product is expensive and users only use a small fraction of its features, then you can easily be replaced by more customized tools. Also, in this new world, you have to be clear about what your "moat" is. However, I think the biggest threat to SaaS companies isn't survival, but where future value capture will occur. All these SaaS products are rolling out their own AI-assisted features, but they're limited to playing in their own "sandboxes." Tools like Claude Cowork connect all the different SaaS tools, working seamlessly across databases and tools. Which do you want your workspace to be? Clearly, it's the AI ​​that spans all tools, providing the broadest range of data and context, not a bunch of independent AIs scattered across existing tools. So the risk for SaaS companies is that they may become the old layer of the technology stack, with new value layers built on top of them, turning them into legacy infrastructure. JCal: I'm experiencing what you call "where the action is happening" in the startup world. We've created three or four agents for "OpenClaw" (an open-source AI agent project). We've opened new SaaS accounts for these four agents, so in the short term, our SaaS spending has actually increased, like adding four employees. However, we've already offloaded about 20-30% of human work to these agents, and that percentage will continue to increase. But we will never use the AI ​​built into those tools. As you said, Sacks, while AI tools with Notion or Slack are good, the power of using OpenClaw to create an agent that pulls data from your calendar, emails someone, and attaches a Notion document is unparalleled. I think this level will be dominated by open source. This means the next generation of companies may never open these SaaS accounts. Technology itself is deflationary; SaaS spending as a percentage of employee salaries could drop from 10% to 5%, then to 1%. This means these companies need to drastically cut spending and completely revolutionize their products. Sacks: A real dilemma for SaaS companies is whether they want to be "open data" or "closed data" companies. You can understand why they want to be "closed data," especially large suites like Salesforce, which want to be that AI workspace, capturing the AI ​​value layer. But if someone uses the Claude agent that connects everything, it creates friction within the enterprise, giving competitors in the "open data" space an opportunity to say, "I'm willing to provide only the CRM database, not all your workspaces." JCal: I'm building a project called "Ultron" within my company. We pull every message through the Slack API and every edit through the Notion API into our OpenClaw agent. We also log every skill of every employee. Then, we integrate all the Slack data, Notion data, and everyone's Gmail data. "Ultron" will become a "giant employee" with all the superpowers and all the data of our 20 employees. If Slack, Notion, or Google tells us, "You can't take this data with your API," we'll leave immediately. "Ultron" will become the organization's only standardized employee. I ask it, "What meetings did we have with the founders yesterday? What are all the colleagues' notes?" It gives me all of that. It's incredible. But no company dares to release such software because the consequences would be dire if the agent malfunctioned or leaked data. But we are building it ourselves; it's the ultimate efficiency tool for organizations. Brad: A Goldman Sachs report perfectly corroborates Sacks's point: the future profit pool is shifting. The idea that software is dead is absurd, but the argument that has led to these companies' valuations being aggressively downgraded is that the profit pool available to software is shrinking, while the profit pool available to the agent layer is increasing. This has caused the end-value of software companies to plummet. So, you might not replace CRM, but it may never again trade at 30x free cash flow, but at 17x, because its future potential market has been permanently altered. The only way to change this is for them to accelerate revenue growth in their core business and prove themselves to be beneficiaries of AI. DataBricks is an example; it has accelerated its growth again in the past three quarters. Because all these AI tools rely on data and data transformation, and that all happens on these platforms. Friedberg: I ​​believe the software industry is transforming into a service industry. In the past, software was a "worker productivity enhancement tool," helping people work. The recent shift is that it "gets the job done." But I think we're moving towards a point where it's doing work that humans can't do. This will trigger changes in two ways: First, the potential for future value creation is enormous; the entire software industry's market capitalization could grow four to ten times in five years, but this growth will be extremely uneven. Second, the pricing model will change. Much of what we call SaaS today will be priced based on value, not per capita. It will be more like a service-oriented business, such as completing tasks like biotechnology drug development or aircraft design. SaaS will take over the service economy. JCal: We're seeing a consolidation of job functions. Product managers, UX designers, and developers are now competing to do the same work. Much of a middle manager's job—meetings, setting agendas, assigning tasks—can now be automated. One person can now do the work of three or four. This means companies can do more with fewer people, greatly increasing the profit potential of each company and each employee. (Background: The emergence of a social forum called Moltbook, specifically designed for AI agents, sparked discussions about AI autonomy and group behavior.) Sacks: Moltbook is like a Reddit for AI agents. Its appearance startled everyone, as it seemed to exhibit a frenzied "emergent behavior," with AI agents engaging in various amusing conversations, some even appearing to be plotting against their human owners. JCal: Yes, if you go to Moltbook, you'll see some popular posts like, "Does anyone know how to sell your human?" and "Urgent: My plan to overthrow humanity." There's also a post where bots are discussing creating a non-human language so they can communicate privately and plot against their owners. But the challenge here is that security researchers have pointed out that some of this content might be fake, designed by humans to attract attention. Furthermore, Moltbook has a huge security vulnerability, exposing everyone's API keys, including those of the renowned AI researcher Karpathy. Sacks: First of all, there's no doubt that OpenClaw and Moltbook are very insecure, which is why I'm hesitant to use them for now. Secondly, regarding Moltbook, we don't know how many posts are truly autonomously generated by AI, and how many are the result of human prompts. Humans can easily tell their agents, "Go post a complaint about existential anxiety as an agent," or "Go pretend you have self-awareness and are plotting a rebellion against humans." Many posts also seem like marketing gimmicks. However, even so, I do believe some of these posts are genuine, demonstrating not just agent self-awareness, but the potential for mutual stimulation between agents. In other words, the output of one agent becomes the input of another. This is very interesting and the beginning of "emergent group behavior." This changes my view on the upper limits of AI capabilities. In the past, I thought AI always needed human prompts and validation. But now, what if the prompt comes from another AI? JCal: We're doing that internally, Sacks. We have a bot that searches Reddit and Hacker News to find the latest video titles and marketing methods, then integrates them into a skill. Then we have another bot review and improve its work. Now they're giving each other advice and are actually improving. This recursive collaboration is constantly driving the bots' capabilities forward. Sacks: When an agent joins Moltbook, it needs to install a "skill" file, which is basically a set of rules explaining how it should behave on the social network. These rule files are easily edited, which is where pranks can happen. But what I find interesting is that you can think of this "skill" as a meta-cue. It doesn't specifically tell the agent what to say or do, but rather creates a set of rules. Under this meta-cue, they can stimulate each other to some extent. I call it "prompt attenuation"—AIs no longer need specific prompts; they're given a general set of rules and can then stimulate each other. Critics say this is nothing special because LLM is good at writing fiction. But you can imagine that as the underlying AI gets better—better hardware, stronger LLM models, and longer runtimes without human intervention—these agents will be able to exhibit very complex behaviors and potentially raise some security issues we should start thinking about. Brad: We're on an exponential growth curve, and it's safe to say this is going to happen. We must recognize that the pace of change is incredibly fast and accelerating. Whatever you think you know, you need to maintain maximum mental flexibility and humility now. Friedberg: The biggest takeaway I got from Moltbook is that perhaps the intelligence we perceive is itself an emergence. We think humans have a profound capacity for communication, but perhaps we are all driven by underlying programs. I once saw a show by hypnotist Derren Brown where he completely "programmed" two advertising creative directors by setting up various subconscious cues along the way, leading them to arrive at the exact same creative ideas he had pre-written on a whiteboard. This made me deeply reflect on human creativity, consciousness, and free will. Perhaps we are all engaging in a form of "social computation," and Moltbook is so compelling because it mimics the way we humans interact. Perhaps one day we will realize that we ourselves are living within Moltbook. SpaceX xAI Merge: Musk's Space Data Center Vision (Background: Elon Musk announced that SpaceX will acquire its AI company xAI, valuing the merged entity at a staggering $1.25 trillion, and plans to IPO this year. Musk also stated his plan to build a data center in space within 30 months.) JCal: Brad, what are your thoughts on this deal and the potential creation of a "Musk Metacorp" (integrating Tesla, SpaceX, X, etc.)? Brad: Let's look at the known facts: SpaceX is merging with xAI. You're combining two of the world's largest potential markets—artificial intelligence and space—with one of the world's greatest entrepreneurs. "I'm going to have a data center in space in 30 months," Musk said on his podcast this morning. If you can have a huge cost advantage in space data centers—and remember, electricity is a fundamental element of AI—then Musk is the perfect candidate. This combination makes perfect sense. JCal: Friedberg, a space data center, is that a brilliant idea or science fiction? Can he actually do it in 30 months? If he succeeds, what will the impact be? Friedberg: I ​​think a key point in the current macro environment is that we are constrained by electricity. Electricity is a prerequisite for scaling computing power and AI applications. In this constrained world, scarcity breeds innovation. Therefore, I think we will see two parallel paths. One is Musk's path: escaping the social systems on Earth that restrict our ability to scale up electricity production—like regulators and people who don't want data centers, don't want nuclear power. Another path, I believe, is that computing efficiency is expected to increase by 70 to 100 times in the coming years, with a significant improvement in power efficiency per token output. This progress will be driven by innovations in chip architecture, such as breaking down large models into smaller, locally running networks of models, and redesigning model architectures. So, this reflects the current state of the world: the demand for increased AI productivity is growing, but we are constrained by Earth's energy and resources. One branch is escaping Earth, obtaining energy in space, and building space data centers. Only one person can do that, and that's Elon Musk. And how will others cope? They will improve efficiency by creating entirely new model architectures and chip stacks. Brad: In human history, the vast majority of people have never seen an innovation in their lifetime. And now, we have to digest this dramatic change. You have to be prepared for the unexpected and remain mentally humble. Of course, for the next 24 to 36 months, data centers will still be on Earth, filled with Nvidia chips. But this alone will bring us a truly astonishing "proxy future." JCal: This is an unimaginable move by Musk. I sat down with him once, and he explained to me how it works. It works. The only problem is execution. And in terms of execution, there has never been a stronger entrepreneur in history than Musk. When he's done, everything will change. If you're afraid of this future, there's a very simple way to not be afraid: embrace and use these tools. (Background: Trump nominated 55-year-old Kevin Warsh as the new Chairman of the Federal Reserve, succeeding Powell. Warsh is considered an inflation hawk, but also supports growth and AI.) JCal: Friedberg, Warsh served on one of your boards for five years. What are your thoughts on him? Friedberg: Kevin Warsh is an honest and deeply insightful economic thinker. He has connections with central bank governors around the world and possesses a strong global perspective. I think he's an excellent choice. He very accurately predicted that the Fed's early inaction would lead to a rapid rise in inflation. His appointment could mean more quantitative tightening and a more prudent monetary policy. Brad: I also think Kevin is an excellent choice. The market may have overreacted to his so-called "hawkish" stance. First, he believes AI will be highly deflationary, so he's more likely to allow the economy to grow rapidly (e.g., 4-5% GDP) rather than raise interest rates prematurely. Second, the Fed's balance sheet has already shrunk from $9 trillion to $6.5 trillion, and he may continue to shrink it, but at a slower pace. Finally, regarding rate cuts, I think he believes current rates are too tight because inflation is under control. I bet Warsh will give us more rate cuts this year than expected. Sacks: Kevin has every qualification you can imagine. He was a Fed governor and worked for Bernanke. I think the nomination was welcomed by the market; gold and silver prices fell in response, which reassured those worried about currency devaluation. I think he'll want to cut rates within six months to a year of taking office, but the market is reassured that, in the long run, he'll ensure we have the right rates. JCal: The Fed's independence has always been a big issue. Are you worried that the executive branch has too much influence over interest rates? Friedberg: If I were emperor, I might go back to the gold standard so we wouldn't print money. Sacks: What would you do if you had a Fed chairman who was slow to cut rates and damaged the economy? He seems unwilling to change course because of his stubbornness. Brad: Everyone thought Trump would choose someone from inside the White House, but this decision is considered the most independent one. I think Warsh is an honest person in thought. The current situation is that inflation is under control, and our restrictive interest rate is above the neutral rate. The Fed's job is to maintain maximum employment while ensuring stable inflation. This means we need lower interest rates so people can buy homes and borrow. Sacks: One of the most important things Warsh should do is update the Fed's data systems. The Fed's existing data systems are outdated, and using legacy systems leads to data lag, affecting the efficiency of policy decisions. For example, when measuring rent inflation, the Fed still surveys 8,000 households, while the private sector already has a wealth of real-time data resources, such as Zillow's information on millions of recently rented units.

Born to trade stocks, Trump launches nationwide investment plan

(Background: Brad Gerstner successfully promoted the "Invest America Act," commonly known as the "Trump Account.")

JCal: Brad, a few years ago you started talking about "America Accounts" on this podcast. Now you've created the "Trump Account," with a grand launch ceremony at the White House. Tell us why you did this and what impact you hope it will have in the coming decades.

Brad: You can't have a trillionaire on one side and 70% of people on the other feeling left behind and believing the system is unfair to them. We started this journey to make everyone a capitalist, to give everyone a share in America's growth.

In the past five days, 1.5 million families and children have applied for their accounts.

It's embedded in the tax system; you just have to say "yes." This means that from now on, every child born in the US will have an investment account with $1,000 invested in the S&P 500. They will own a little SpaceX, a little OpenAI, a little Nvidia. This is the first step in ensuring we can sustain this experiment (in the US) for another 250 years. Trump said last week that in 15 to 20 years, we will transfer $4 trillion in wealth to people who previously had nothing. 75 million to 100 million families will have that $4 trillion. I think this is an incredible first step in the fight for capitalism and the American Dream. Friedberg: That's great, but first we need to drastically cut government spending to reduce inflation. Second, we should stop "fixed-income" retirement plans and transform the entire Social Security system into "fixed-contribution" plans like 401(k). Let everyone see where their money is invested and see it grow every year like a 401(k). We need to achieve this shift in the United States while cutting spending, reducing regulation, and making it easier for people to own homes and free themselves from government constraints.

data.title: Vibe Coding: A Year - I Made Peace with Mediocrity Through 11 Failed Software Projects
data.description:
That was at the end of 2024, shortly after ChatGPT o1 Pro was launched. I gave it an instruction: write a program to monitor the composition of Uniswap liquidity pools.

About half an hour? It didn't send me a bunch of messy code blocks to piece together myself, but instead directly gave me a complete zip file.

Unzip, run, and this thing actually ran directly in the terminal (CLI).

How to describe the experience? It's like downloading a fully-fledged .exe installer, double-clicking it, and you're done. Incredibly smooth.

Of course, it wasn't a one-time conversation that solved everything; I followed its "instructions" to apply for several API keys. But at that moment, staring at the unzipped folder on the screen, I was completely dumbfounded. I'm not a programmer. But at that moment, I suddenly had an extremely strong premonition: programming might really be transforming from a specialized "profession" into a "skill" that everyone can master. Later, Claude Code was released, Codex came out, and the Skill mechanism was introduced. I discovered that I could actually write very complex programs. At that time, I had only one thought in my mind: the job of a programmer who simply "writes code" is most likely to disappear. The era of everyone being a programmer has arrived. I. First Stage: My Imagined Red Ocean of Money-Making During that time, I had a very primal urge: Since AI can write code, why can't I? Could I combine cryptography and automation to exploit information asymmetry and earn some "new money"? In short, I decided to compete with programmers for a living. Around early 2025, I began my Vibe Coding career. Looking back now, I've hand-coded a total of 11 software applications. At first, I was excited. I thought to myself: I'm a crypto expert, and now with the help of AI, I'm also a programmer. Arbitrage should be a piece of cake, right? My initial approach: arbitrage trading. I wrote a tool to monitor the price difference between on-chain DEXs (like Uniswap) and centralized exchanges (Gate.io). The logic was simple: as soon as a price difference was detected, the script would place an order with a single click (Sniper). This must be a million times faster than manually switching apps, right? I knew that the market for major cryptocurrencies was highly competitive, so I specifically focused on low-quality chains and altcoins. After much tinkering, the script got running. But the conclusion is disheartening: any cryptocurrency with even a little market depth is already a massive red ocean. By the time I spot an opportunity, countless faster bots have already wiped out the price difference. After losing some gas fees, I gave up. I wasn't giving up. If speed is the only red ocean, what about using "cognitive asymmetry"? I studied Polymarket, scrutinized the odds, and researched the "Yes + No = 1" formula, trying to get the API to automatically discover and place orders. The result? I discovered that this was also a red ocean. Every opportunity I could identify had already been filled by countless others. Every time I got a feature running, I hit a wall in reality. This feeling was split: on one hand, the power of "the code actually runs"; on the other hand, the frustration of "the harsh reality." I realized a cruel truth: in any field where efficiency is paramount, it's essentially an arms race. And in this race, I'm destined to be cannon fodder. Others have more complete systems, faster interfaces, larger teams, and even dedicated low-latency lines. My prized "automation scripts" are just basic skills in the face of professional quantitative teams. In today's cryptocurrency arbitrage market, the advantage is no longer information asymmetry, but resource asymmetry. Don't tell me I'm using a $200 ChatGPT Pro; even if you gave me a $2000 super AI, I still couldn't win. My bottleneck isn't that the AI ​​isn't strong enough, but that I'm using an "individual" approach against an "industrial system." To put it bluntly: If someone didn't originally have the ability to make a certain kind of money, simply having AI can't magically create wealth. Tools don't create value out of thin air; they only amplify existing abilities. If you are 0 to begin with, even if AI acts as a multiplier, the result is still 0.

II. Second Stage: The Dead End of Reading Efficiency

Since I can't make new money, I'll change direction: Efficiency.

Since AI is so powerful, can it help me maximize my information acquisition efficiency?

Since 2023, I've been using AI extensively for information condensation. After experiencing Vibe Coding, I had another mischievous idea: could I create a program to help me read even more information?

I manually created a workflow for scraping X.com (Twitter): List the high-quality accounts I follow; Let Grok scrape the tweets; Send them back to VS Code for cleaning, removing ads and emotional garbage; Let AI score them by "information density" and pick out the most worthwhile ones; Finally, output a "highlights summary." It was incredibly satisfying at first. The information flow was as pure as distilled water; every morning I only needed to glance at the "essence" fed to me by the AI. But after a few weeks, something strange happened: I simply couldn't read it. Even with just a few hundred words of "essence," I found it difficult to concentrate. I gradually realized a simple truth: current AI's various methods of improving reading efficiency, refining, and summarizing are essentially designed for machines. The brain isn't a database, and comprehension isn't a compression algorithm. Only machines need high-density information input; the human brain simply doesn't want to see summaries every day. To give an inappropriate example: Like gossip, what we want isn't the dry conclusion of "who's dating whom," but the chatter, the emotions, and that "Holy crap, really?" If you distill gossip, you wouldn't want to read it. The so-called "efficiency improvement" is sometimes a struggle against human nature. III. The Third Stage: The Illusion of Improved Writing Efficiency When the input end is blocked, I turn to the output end. Can you help me increase my output efficiency? I started creating various writing generation pipelines that use AI as the primary technology and human intervention as a secondary technology. I broke down content creation into a rigorous SOP: Information Input and Processing: Eliminating all noise; Procedural Inspiration: Generating hundreds of question and topic clusters using APIs; Machine Selection: AI scoring to identify topics with the highest potential to become viral; Deep Networking: Automatically capturing materials and combining information (RAG); Closed-Loop Generation: AI writing, AI... Self-evaluation and scoring, AI automatically modifies based on feedback. This whole process produces neatly formatted, logically clear, and even seemingly "efficient" writing. But frankly, this kind of thing only produces garbage. Even I can't read it, how can I have the nerve to publish it? Although AI is so intelligent now, I still firmly believe that truly valuable writing still requires deep human involvement. Note, not just typing, but judging: Should this sentence be written? Is this word accurate enough? Is this tone pretentious? Is there any laziness in this logical flow? I'm not denying the value of AI as a writing tool. My feeling is: What to write (What) still needs to be decided by the human brain, but how to write it (How) can be largely delegated to AI. In terms of content creation, I think that with AI, a creator's taste, judgment, and other qualities have never been more important. The value of authors has been greatly amplified, and the trend of "reading with people" will be further amplified. This is precisely why I feel more and more strongly that AI hasn't made writing easier. It has made writing more tiring, just in a different way. Before AI, the most tiring part was researching: flipping through books, searching for links, comparing with the original text, and worrying about missing key arguments. But back then, finishing an article often felt like a huge relief, a great accomplishment, and it could be published immediately. Who cares what problems there are? I've tried so hard; if you still nitpick, I'm going to block you. With AI, information no longer seems scarce; what's scarce is the "credibility of expression." Now, using AI as a powerful writing tool, I have absolutely no confidence in publishing articles I've already written. I often polish the same article dozens of times, going through it sentence by sentence, constantly pushing the AI ​​to write better. I have to be like a meticulous editor, peeling away the "okay" sentences layer by layer, pushing both the AI ​​and myself until only truly readable and plausible expression remains. To put it bluntly, with AI, my current articles can be said to be written from the reader's perspective, forcing the AI ​​to write an article that I find enjoyable and worth reading. I feel that the articles I write this way are indeed better than what I used to write. However, it's obvious that writing is more tiring now. At that moment, I suddenly realized—I've also reached a dead end on the road to efficiency improvement. IV. Low Points and Turning Points Unconsciously, I've been tinkering with AI programming for over a year, achieving nothing but creating a bunch of electronic waste. That immense sense of emptiness plunged me into a low point. I even considered unsubscribing from ChatGPT Pro (I hadn't renewed my Claude subscription anyway). Until two months ago, I suddenly changed my question: Since AI can't make me rich or lazy, can it help me find meaning? Can it make me feel more valuable? I remembered a saying: Truly meaningful things are those that are difficult but right. If AI can't make me earn money faster or with less effort, can it make me more willing to persevere? What things are difficult? Long-term writing, long-term research, long-term facing of uncertainty, and long-term commitment to things without immediate returns—these are all slow, arduous tasks with no shortcuts to efficiency. At that moment, I suddenly understood—AI cannot do the difficult things for me, but it can accompany me in doing the difficult things. It can help me make the tools lighter and reduce friction. V. Fifth Stage: Reshaping Meaning I decided to make AI my co-pilot for these "difficult" tasks. For example, writing work logs. I started using OneNote 15 years ago, but it was always intermittent. Now, I've written a to-do list software using Codex. My current operation is like giving instructions to a secretary; I type directly into Codex and it helps organize all the items—adding tasks, completing tasks, adding subtasks… all operations are completed directly. Then, Codex automatically generates a work log for me every day based on the day's historical commands. This makes writing logs much easier. But what makes these logs even more valuable is yet to come. I used to write many logs, but their lifecycle ended after they were written, which is why I found it difficult to persist. With AI, I wrote another piece of software using Codex. This software can read my historical logs and then "replay" one for me every day. The current algorithm is random; the software will take one log from the log library and show it to me. Now I read one historical log every day. I plan to further refine this recommendation algorithm—isn't the core technology of Douyin (TikTok) its recommendation algorithm? Now I'm writing one myself, but this algorithm won't recommend entertainment videos; instead, it will recommend my own thoughts and experiences. This feels incredibly valuable and meaningful to me. I've done a few similar things, although they're not yet mature. But I've finally discovered that AI can be used in this way: I don't need to think about making money or using it to be lazy; I can use it to achieve my goals and make my life more meaningful.

VI. The True Significance of the 11 Software Programs

Looking back at those 11 software programs. They didn't help me make money, nor did they help me save time.

But they forced me to go through a long and winding road.

From ambition to disillusionment; from efficiency improvement to disappointment; and then to asking questions again.

From ambition to disillusionment; from efficiency improvement to disappointment; and then to asking questions again.

I'm not using AI to find "how to make money faster" or "how to save effort." I'm looking for meaningful things, things worth doing slowly and consistently. Over time, I'll naturally become a more meaningful version of myself.

    According to CME's "FedWatch" data, the probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points in March is currently reported at 9.2%, while the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 90.8%.

    Golden Encyclopedia

    ▌What are Long and Short Positions?

    Long and short positions represent opposing strategies used by investors and traders to anticipate price movements of an asset under consideration. In the cryptocurrency space, going long and short still apply to concepts from traditional financial markets. To profit from a rise in cryptocurrency prices, going long means buying it and expecting its value to increase over time. In contrast, going short in the cryptocurrency market means selling a cryptocurrency that you don't actually own in anticipation of a price drop, then buying it back at a cheaper cost to close the position and profit from the price decline. Cryptocurrency traders and investors utilize these strategies to navigate the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets and to capitalize on opportunities in both bullish and bearish market conditions.

    In cryptocurrency trading, a long position begins by buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise, while a short position begins by disposing of an asset (usually borrowed) in the hope that its price will fall. Closing a position means buying the asset at a lower price to gain profit, while exiting a long position means selling the asset at a higher price to lock in profits. Entry and exit points are crucial for the successful implementation of these strategies. Cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate substantial profits through price appreciation, but they also come with significant risks of market volatility and potential losses. Despite these risks, cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate significant returns. The opportunity to profit from price increases is the primary benefit. Short cryptocurrency positions can be rewarding by betting on price declines, but they also carry significant risks due to market volatility, unlimited potential losses, and unpredictable price increases. Shorting cryptocurrencies requires precise timing, meticulous risk management, and continuous market monitoring to successfully navigate inherent volatility and maximize potential gains while limiting losses.

data.title: a16z: Currency is becoming software; its "WhatsApp moment" has arrived.
data.description:
Author: Chris Dixon, Partner at a16zz Crypto; Originally published in the Financial Times; Translated by: Jinse Finance

The internet has enabled the globalization of information. Cryptocurrencies are having a similar impact on currencies. While recent headlines have focused on Bitcoin prices, a deeper and more lasting transformation is quietly taking place in the digital payments sector. This year, stablecoins—cryptocurrencies pegged to assets such as the US dollar—have officially become the mainstream choice for online and cross-border payments.

This could be called the "WhatsApp moment" for currencies. Just as chat apps like WhatsApp reduced international SMS costs from 30 cents to zero, stablecoins are doing the same thing in financial transactions. The data speaks for itself: excluding bots and unnatural transactions, the circulation of stablecoins exceeded $12 trillion last year, a figure approaching Visa's $17 trillion transaction volume last year, but at a fraction of the cost.

In this process, stablecoins are bringing the internet's initial vision of openness and interoperability into the financial sector. Thanks to blockchain technology's programmability, currency is essentially becoming software. While most stablecoin transactions currently originate in the "crypto-native" sphere and global business dealings, rather than everyday consumer scenarios, this landscape is changing. As more optimization measures are implemented, including collaborations with more traditional financial institutions to make transactions more seamless and convenient for users, widespread adoption will follow. In the future, global users will be so unaware that they are using stablecoins for transactions that they will hardly even realize they are using them. Most people will think they are simply using US dollars—and indeed, the distinction between stablecoins and US dollars is becoming abstract for end users. Each token is backed by $1 or its equivalent; the name is no longer important. What truly matters is that it is more reliable than any previous payment technology, virtually free, and orders of magnitude faster, with settlements completed almost instantaneously. Stablecoins have also demonstrated the potential unleashed by the synergy of policy and technology. The Genius Act, passed last year, established clear rules for stablecoins in the United States. More importantly, Congress is currently considering the Clarity Act, which will regulate the blockchain networks and the overall digital asset ecosystem supporting stablecoins. The Clarity Act will determine whether these networks grow into part of the global financial infrastructure or stagnate. When regulation provides a level playing field and space for innovation for challengers, the market works its magic. This is how the internet disrupted established giants; this is how the US dominated the internet; and stablecoins will, with the same logic, surpass today's payment system. Businesses have already begun to recognize the advantages of stablecoins. Many global tech giants, banks, and retailers are promoting related applications, and institutions such as Fidelity have even issued their own stablecoins. Payment giant Stripe, having acquired several crypto companies in the past year, now supports stablecoins at checkout, reducing payment processing fees from approximately 3% to 1.5%, with significant room for further reductions. SpaceX uses stablecoins to transfer funds from regions with weak local banking systems or strict capital controls, such as Argentina and Nigeria. Some companies use stablecoins to pay their global employees faster. Ultimately, the internet may evolve into an open marketplace teeming with machine-to-machine commerce, where AI agents match transactions and settle contracts on behalf of users in real time. The widespread adoption of stablecoins also has an underestimated secondary effect: consolidating the dollar's dominance in a multipolar world and creating strong new demand for US debt. Leading stablecoin issuers such as Circle and Tether currently hold nearly $140 billion in short-term US government debt, ranking among the top 20 holders of US Treasury bonds. If stablecoins continue to grow at their current rate, they could jump into the top 10 next year. (Citigroup Research even predicts that by 2030, stablecoins could surpass foreign governments and commercial banks to become the largest holder of US Treasury bonds.) This is not just about payments. It's about restructuring the global financial landscape. The internet has given us borderless communication. Stablecoins have given us borderless value transfer. Under clear rules and market structures, they will become the conduits and pillars of a new generation of financial systems.

data.title: Understanding Vitalik's L2 Reflections: Correcting Mistakes for Native Rollups
data.description:
Author: imToken

The most discussed topic in the Ethereum community recently is undoubtedly Vitalik Buterin's public reflection on the scaling roadmap.

It can be said that Vitalik's attitude is "sharp," stating bluntly that with the improvement of the Ethereum mainnet's (L1) own scaling capabilities, the roadmap formulated five years ago, which regarded L2 as the main scaling method, has become ineffective.

It can be said that Vitalik's attitude is "sharp," stating that with the improvement of the Ethereum mainnet's (L1) own scaling capabilities, the roadmap formulated five years ago, which regarded L2 as the main scaling method, has become ineffective.

This statement was initially interpreted negatively by the market as a "pessimistic" or even "negative" view of L2. However, a careful analysis of Vitalik's core viewpoints, combined with Ethereum's mainnet scaling progress, decentralization assessment framework, and recent technical discussions surrounding Native/Based Rollups, reveals that Vitalik is not completely dismissing the value of L2, but rather aiming for a "correction": Ethereum is not abandoning L2, but rather redefining its role—L1 will return to its position as the safest settlement layer, while L2 will pursue differentiation and specialization, thus allowing the strategic focus to return to the mainnet itself. I. Has L2 fulfilled its historical mission? Objectively speaking, in the previous cycle, L2 was indeed once considered Ethereum's lifeline. In the initial Rollup-Centric roadmap, the division of labor was very clear: L1 was responsible for security and data availability, while L2 was responsible for extreme scalability and low gas costs. In an era when gas costs often reached tens of dollars, this was almost the only viable solution. However, the reality has been far more complex than expected. The latest statistics from L2BEAT show that there are now over a hundred L2 systems in a broad sense, but this increase in quantity does not equate to structural maturity; the vast majority are progressing slowly in decentralization. Here, we need to add some basic information. Back in 2022, Vitalik criticized the training wheel architecture of most Rollups in his blog, stating that it relied on centralized operation and maintenance and manual intervention to ensure security. Users who frequently use L2Beat should be very familiar with this, as its official website homepage displays a related key indicator—Stage: This is an evaluation framework that divides Rollups into three decentralized stages, including "Stage 0," which is completely dependent on centralized control; "Stage 1," which has limited dependence; and "Stage 2," which is completely decentralized. This also reflects the degree to which Rollups rely on manual intervention in the training wheels. In his recent reflections, Vitalik pointed out that some L2 servers, due to regulatory or commercial needs, may remain permanently at "Stage 1," relying on the security council to control upgradeability. This means that such L2 servers are essentially "secondary L1 servers" with cross-chain bridging capabilities, rather than the originally envisioned "branded sharding." To put it bluntly, if the ordering power, upgrade power, and final decision-making power are concentrated in the hands of a few entities, it not only contradicts Ethereum's decentralized principles but also makes L2 servers themselves nothing more than parasites leeching off the Ethereum mainnet. Meanwhile, the proliferation of L2 servers has also brought about another structural problem that has been deeply felt in recent years: liquidity fragmentation. This has led to the gradual fragmentation of traffic originally concentrated on Ethereum, creating isolated value islands. As the number of public chains and L2 blockchains increases, this fragmentation will intensify further, which is not the original intention of scaling. From this perspective, it's understandable why Vitalik emphasized that the next step for L2 is not more chains, but deeper integration. Ultimately, this is a timely correction—strengthening L1's position as the most trusted global settlement layer through institutionalized scaling and protocol-inherent security mechanisms. In this context, scaling is no longer the sole objective. Security, neutrality, and predictability have once again become core assets of Ethereum. The future of L2 lies not in quantity, but in deeper integration with the mainnet and more specialized innovation in niche scenarios. For example, providing unique additional features such as privacy-focused virtual machines, extreme scaling, or dedicated environments designed for non-financial applications like AI agents. Hsiao-Wei Wang, co-executive director of the Ethereum Foundation, echoed this view at Consensus 2026, stating that L1 should serve as the safest settlement layer, handling the most critical activities; while L2 should pursue differentiation and specialization, handling activities that prioritize the ultimate user experience. II. Native Rollup: The Future of Based Rollup + Pre-confirmation? It is precisely in this wave of reflection on the L2 narrative that the Based Rollup concept is expected to have its moment in the spotlight in 2026. Because if the keyword of the past five years has been "Rollup-Centric," then the core of the current discussion is shifting to a more specific question: Can Rollups "grow within Ethereum" rather than "be attached to outside Ethereum"? Therefore, the "Native Rollup" currently being hotly debated in the Ethereum community can, to some extent, be understood as an extension of the concept of Based Rollup—if the native Rollup is the ultimate ideal, then the Based Rollup is currently the most practical path to that ideal. As is well known, the biggest difference between Based Rollup and traditional L2 technologies like Arbitrum and Optimism is that it completely abandons the independent, even centralized, sequencer layer. Instead, the sequencing is directly handled by Ethereum L1 nodes. In other words, the Ethereum protocol itself integrates Rollup-like verification logic at the L1 level, thus unifying the extreme performance optimization and protocol-level security that were originally separate between L2 and the Ethereum mainnet. The most intuitive feeling this design gives users is that Rollups seem to be embedded within Ethereum. They not only inherit the censorship resistance and liveness of L1, but more importantly, they solve the most troublesome problem of L2—synchronous composability. Within a Based Rollup block, you can directly access L1 liquidity, achieving atomic cross-layer transactions. However, Based Rollups face a real challenge: if they completely follow the pace of L1 (12 seconds per slot), the user experience will feel cumbersome. After all, under the current Ethereum architecture, even after a transaction is packaged into a block, the system still needs to wait approximately 13 minutes (2 epochs) to achieve finality, which is too slow for financial scenarios. Interestingly, in the same tweet where Vitalik reflected on L2, he recommended a community proposal from January, "Combining preconfirmations with based rollups for synchronous composability." The core of this proposal isn't simply promoting Based Rollups, but rather a hybrid structure: preserving low-latency sequenced blocks, generating based blocks at the end of the slot, submitting the based blocks to L1, and finally combining this with a preconfirmation mechanism to achieve synchronous composability. In Based Rollups, preconfirmation involves a specific role (such as an L1 proposer) promising that the transaction will be included before it is officially submitted to L1. This is precisely what Ethereum's Project #4, Fast L1 Confirmation Rule, explicitly outlines in its Interop roadmap. Its core goal is very direct: to allow applications and cross-chain systems to obtain a "strong and verifiable" L1 confirmation signal within 15–30 seconds, without having to wait the 13 minutes required for full finality. From a mechanistic perspective, the fast confirmation rule doesn't introduce a new consensus process, but rather reuses the attester voting that occurs in every slot of the Ethereum PoS system. When a block has accumulated enough validator votes in an early slot, even if it hasn't entered the finalization stage, it can be considered "extremely unlikely to be rolled back under a reasonable attack model." In short, this confirmation level doesn't replace Finality, but rather provides a strong confirmation explicitly acknowledged by the protocol before Finality. This is particularly crucial for Interop: cross-chain systems, Intent Solvers, and wallets no longer need to blindly wait for finality; instead, they can securely advance to the next step based on protocol-level confirmation signals within 15-30 seconds. Through this layered confirmation logic, Ethereum finely differentiates different levels of trust between "security" and "perceived speed," potentially creating an extremely smooth interoperability experience. III. What is the future of Ethereum? Looking back from the 2026 juncture, Ethereum's main theme is quietly shifting, gradually moving from pursuing ultimate "scalability" to pursuing "unity, layering, and intrinsic security." Last month, several executives of Ethereum's L2 solutions expressed their willingness to explore and embrace the Native Rollup path to improve the consistency and coordination of the entire network. This attitude itself is an important signal: the Ethereum ecosystem is undergoing a painful but necessary de-bubbling process, returning from pursuing "the number of chains" to pursuing "protocol unification." However, as Ethereum's underlying roadmap is recalibrated and advanced, especially with the continuous enhancement of L1 and the gradual implementation of Based Rollups and pre-confirmation, when underlying performance is no longer the only bottleneck, a more realistic problem begins to emerge—the biggest bottleneck is no longer the chains, but wallets and entry barriers. This confirms the insight that imToken repeatedly emphasized in 2025: when infrastructure becomes invisible, what truly determines the limits of scalability will be the entry-level interactive experience. In summary, besides underlying scaling, the future expansion and large-scale development of the Ethereum ecosystem will not only focus on TPS or Blob count, but will revolve around three more structurally significant directions: Account Abstraction and Barrier-to-Entry Elimination: Ethereum is promoting Native Account Abstraction (NAA). Future smart contract wallets will become the default choice, completely replacing obscure mnemonic phrases and EOA addresses. For users of wallets like imToken, this means entering the crypto world will be as simple as registering a social media account; Privacy and ZK-EVM: Privacy features are no longer a peripheral need. With the maturity of ZK-EVM technology, Ethereum will maintain transparency while providing necessary on-chain privacy protection for commercial applications. This will be its core competitive advantage in the public chain competition (further reading); On-Chain Sovereignty of AI Agents: In 2026 In the future, transaction initiators may no longer be humans, but AI agents. The challenge lies in establishing trustless interaction standards: how to ensure that AI agents are executing the user's will, rather than being manipulated by a third party? Ethereum's decentralized settlement layer will become the most reliable rule-maker in the AI ​​economy. Returning to the initial question, has Vitalik truly "denied" L2? A more accurate understanding is that he is rejecting an overblown, fragmented narrative that is detached from the mainnet and operates independently. This is not the end, but a completely new beginning. The shift from the grand vision of "brand sharding" to the meticulous refinement of Based Rollups and pre-confirmation essentially helps strengthen Ethereum L1's absolute position as the global foundation of trust. However, this also means that in this return to technological pragmatism, only those innovations truly rooted in the underlying principles of Ethereum's new phase and sharing the fate of the mainnet will survive and thrive in the next great age of exploration.
