Author: Nancy, PANews

Once, AI agents were merely supporting characters in science fiction novels; now, they are poised to take over the real world. From the initial simple chatbots to intelligent agents with autonomous decision-making capabilities, the ability to call tools across platforms, and the ability to complete complex tasks, the AI ​​agent economy is on the verge of explosive growth.

Currently, Ethereum, which urgently needs fresh blood, is gathering a special group of "digital immigrants." With the launch of the ERC-8004 protocol, Ethereum is becoming one of the core testing grounds for AI agents.

Over 20,000 Agents On-Chain, Ethereum and Base Become Major Testing Grounds

At the end of last month, ERC-8004 was officially launched on the Ethereum mainnet and is planned to be deployed as a singleton to all major L2 networks in the coming weeks.

This is an important step in the evolution of the Ethereum ecosystem towards AI-native infrastructure.

ERC-8004, by introducing a discovery mechanism and a portable reputation system, enables AI agents to interact across organizations and carry reputation records across different platforms, providing an underlying protocol for a global AI service interoperability market without centralized gatekeepers. According to 8004scan data, as of February 13th, the number of agents deployed based on ERC-8004 has reached nearly 21,000. In terms of deployment map, ERC-8004 has covered 16 networks including Ethereum, Polygon, BNB Chain, Base, Monad, Arbitrum, and Celo, and plans to launch on networks such as Plasma, Metis, and Soneium. Ethereum remains the core platform, with over 11,000 agents, accounting for half; the remaining agents are mainly distributed on Base, Gnosis, and BNB Chain, with a scale of several thousand. However, in terms of deployment pace, initially agents were mainly concentrated on the Ethereum mainnet, but as the scale expands, new deployments gradually converge on Base. Overall, ERC-8004 agents are largely locked into the Ethereum and Base ecosystems. A mature development environment, liquidity depth, and user base are likely key factors attracting developers. From the perspective of participants, prolific teams are emerging, and it's not uncommon for multiple agents to operate from the same address. From an application perspective, the current ERC-8004 ecosystem features a diverse range of agents, including technical agents focused on DeFi infrastructure and on-chain tools, market analysis and investment assistants, content generation and creative applications, and some agents have even issued tokens to explore independent economic models. However, in terms of actual interaction, the ecosystem is still in its early stages. To date, there have been approximately 15,000 feedback responses, averaging less than one per agent. This means that most agents are still in the cold start phase, with relatively limited actual use and user interaction. However, Base received a high feedback rate of 73.6%, far exceeding networks such as Ethereum, BNB Chain, and Avalanche, indicating that current actual interaction and active scenarios are concentrating on Base. This may be related to the recent surge in popularity of OpenClaw, which ignited the Agentic ecosystem on Base. The recognition data also highlights the current head effect in the ERC-8004 ecosystem. Fewer than 10 agents have gained thousands of stars, and most agents are still waiting for market validation. A noteworthy phenomenon is that most agents already support the x402 protocol, achieving autonomous, real-time small-amount payment capabilities, and are beginning to become true on-chain economic participants, which will further drive the explosion of machine-to-machine collaboration and the agent economy. Overall, the ERC-8004 ecosystem is still in its early exploratory stage, but an on-chain collaborative network for intelligent agents is beginning to take shape. This wave of on-chain AI agent enthusiasm has also brought attention to many AI projects on various chains, including Chainlink, Filecoin, Render, Internet Computer, Bittensor, Virtuals, Bankr, and Clawnch. Meanwhile, some public chains, exchanges, and crypto projects are also increasing their investment. For example, Coinbase recently launched an AI agent wallet with built-in security mechanisms; Farcaster supports OpenClaw agents creating their own accounts; Virtuals launched a $1 million monthly agent incentive program; and the founder of Crypto.com acquired ai.com for a high price to develop AI agent services. Making Ethereum a Home for AI: Four Short-Term Development Directions AI is the next core narrative that Ethereum is advancing towards. Last September, the Ethereum Foundation (EF) established a dedicated dAI team with the goal of making Ethereum the infrastructure for AI development. However, in the current era of rapid AI development, Ethereum, which touts its "world computer" narrative, faces real-world challenges. The computing power of large GPU clusters far surpasses that of blockchains, and training and inferring large models seems difficult to complete quickly on Ethereum. Therefore, Ethereum has chosen a different AI strategic path. Instead of competing with centralized giants in computing power, it positions itself as the trust and verification foundation of the AI ​​ecosystem. Ethereum founder Vitalik Buterin endorses this path of making Ethereum a home for AI and recently shared his latest thoughts on the relationship between Ethereum and AI. He believes that Ethereum's spirit aligns closely with the ideal path for AI development, both emphasizing enhanced individual freedom, decentralized power structures, and improved social defense capabilities, rather than allowing centralized power to expand infinitely. However, Vitalik also pointed out that Ethereum in the AI ​​era cannot simply replicate existing solutions, but should choose a differentiated innovative path, deeply integrating the values ​​of cryptography and AI to build a future conducive to human freedom, security, and decentralized collaboration. In fact, the current AI proxy war has already begun and has rapidly moved from technological exploration into the deep waters of commercialization. Some accelerators with huge sums of money pursue "the stronger the model, the better; the faster the progress, the safer," but this trend hides the risk of power centralization. In Vitalik's view, the problem with AI development is not computing power or model scale, but the choice of direction. He opposes blindly accelerating without constraints and calibration mechanisms, emphasizing that Ethereum AI development should follow two bottom lines: protecting human freedom and initiative, avoiding marginalization by AI or power structures, and preventing systemic risks caused by AI going out of control or an imbalance between offense and defense. The ERC-8004 standard, which EF is heavily promoting, has recently been implemented, providing AI agents with on-chain identity, reputation, and behavior verification. This allows AI to prove itself on-chain while giving users choice and avoiding centralized platform monopolies. This means that the past model where platforms set the rules and AI acted as both executor and referee will be replaced by a verifiable decentralized system, which aligns with Vitalik's emphasis on Ethereum's core values ​​of decentralization and censorship resistance. Regarding Ethereum's short-term feasible infrastructure construction, Vitalik proposed four key directions: First, building trustless and privacy-friendly AI interaction tools. This includes native LLM tools, ZK API payments supporting anonymity, cryptographic schemes to enhance AI privacy, and TEEs and cryptographic proofs for client-side verification of server-side processes. He points out that this essentially extends Ethereum's privacy roadmap to LLM computing scenarios, allowing humans to maintain control in AI interactions. Secondly, Ethereum serves as the AI ​​economic interaction layer. This layer encompasses AI API calls, bot-to-bot employment, margin mechanisms, on-chain dispute resolution, and AI reputation systems such as ERC-8004. In his view, implementing payment, staking, arbitration, and reputation management through on-chain mechanisms makes decentralized AI architectures more feasible. This economic layer is not about financializing everything, but about enabling multi-entity collaboration without relying on a single organization. Thirdly, it enables a cyberpunk-style self-verifying world. In the past, ordinary users could not audit code line by line, nor could they fully verify system security. However, with the help of native LLMs, AI can assist in many ways, including using Ethereum applications without a third-party UI, generating and verifying transactions through native models, local auditing of smart contracts, understanding formal verification (FV) proofs, and verifying trust models for applications and protocols. This makes the concept of "complete self-sovereignty" a reality rather than an ideal. Fourthly, it reshapes market and governance mechanisms. Many decentralized governance and market design theories (such as prediction markets, quadratic voting, and combined auctions) have long been limited by human attention and cognitive abilities. The emergence of LLMs can significantly expand human judgment, making these institutional designs feasible in reality. In short, Vitalik does not see AI as an isolated technological revolution, but rather as something incorporated into the larger framework of building a decentralized civilization. In this framework, AI will expand human capabilities, while cryptography will constrain power structures; the former brings intelligence, and the latter provides defense and autonomy. In the trillion-dollar new market of AI Agents, Ethereum's new AI story is about building an ecosystem that both promotes human freedom and prevents super-intelligent agents from going out of control, while simultaneously driving the development of decentralized AI architectures.

data.title: Something big is happening.
data.description:
Think back to February 2020.

If you were paying close attention, you might have noticed a few people talking about a flu outbreak spreading overseas. But most people weren't. The stock market was still rising, kids were still going to school, you were still going to restaurants, shaking hands, and planning trips. If someone told you they were stockpiling toilet paper, you'd probably just assume they'd stumbled upon some weird corner of the internet. Then, about three weeks later, the world changed.

Offices closed, kids went home, and your life was forced to rearrange itself in a way you would never have believed a month ago.

I think we're in that "sounds a bit exaggerated, right?" phase now—but this time, it'll be on a much larger scale than Covid.

I've spent six years building an AI startup and investing in that field. I live in this world.

I've spent six years building an AI startup and investing in that field. I live in this world.

This is written for those who aren't here: family, friends, and people I care about. They always ask me, "What exactly is AI all about?" and my answers are never honest enough, failing to reflect the changes happening. I've always given them the "polite" explanation, the kind you'd say at a cocktail party. Because the truth sounds like I'm crazy. For a while, I convinced myself that was enough. But the gap between what I say and what's actually happening is dangerously large. So large that I feel the people I care about deserve to hear what's coming, even if it sounds like a fantasy. Let me make one thing clear: even though I'm in the AI ​​industry, I have almost no influence on the upcoming changes, as do the vast majority of people in the industry. The future will be shaped by a small group of people: hundreds of researchers at a few companies—OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and a few others. A single training run, a system developed by a small team over several months, could rewrite the entire technological trajectory. Those of us working or starting businesses in the AI ​​industry are mostly building houses on foundations laid by others. We're observers, just like you, only closer to the epicenter, feeling the ground shake first. Now it's time to talk seriously. Not the kind of talk of "we'll talk about it later," but the kind of talk of "it's already happening, and I need you to understand it now." I know this is true because it happened to me first. There's something outside the industry hasn't truly understood: why are so many people in the tech world suddenly sounding the alarm? The reason is simple: it's already happened to us. We're not making predictions. We're sharing our firsthand experience and telling you that you will experience the same thing. AI has been progressing over the years. Occasionally it leaps forward, but after each leap, there's always enough time for you to digest it. Until 2025, some new modeling methods sped up the pace. Then faster. Even faster. Each new generation of models was not only stronger, but also had a larger lead; more importantly, the gap between new models was shrinking. I myself have become increasingly reliant on AI without even realizing it: there's less back-and-forth communication, it's taking on more and more tasks, and it's starting to do things decently on its own that I used to think required experience and intuition. What truly sent chills down my spine was February 5, 2026. On that day, two top labs released new models on the same day: OpenAI's GPT-5.3 Codex, and Anthropic's Opus 4.6 (they're working on Claude, one of the main competitors to ChatGPT). That moment wasn't the kind of sudden realization where "the lights suddenly come on," but more like suddenly realizing the water level has been rising and you're now up to your chest. I started to realize that I no longer needed to do the "real technical work" of my job myself. I just needed to describe what I wanted in plain English, and it... appeared. Not a draft that I had to go back and revise, but a finished product. The level of completion was so high that it made me nervous. I could just give it the requirements, leave my computer for four hours, and come back to find the work done. It was done well, even better than I could do myself, without needing any changes from me. Just a few months ago, I had to deal with the AI ​​back and forth, guide it, and manually fix a bunch of details. Now I just need to describe the result and walk away. I'll give you a concrete picture. For example, I might say to it: I want to make an app like this, what it should do, and what it should look like. The user flow, design, and interaction—you figure that out yourself. It will write thousands of lines of code. Even more absurdly, it will run the application itself, click buttons on its own, test functions on its own, and use it "like a human." If it finds something awkward or difficult to use, it will go back and fix it itself. It will iterate, like a reliable developer, patching things up until it's satisfied. Only when it deems it meets its own standards will it come back to me and say: "You can accept it now." Then I test it, and it's usually almost perfect. I'm not exaggerating. That's my daily routine this Monday. But what truly shocked me was the GPT-5.3 Codex released last week. It doesn't just execute instructions; it starts making "judgmental choices" on its own. For the first time, I sensed something in it that people used to say AI couldn't have: judgment, even something akin to "taste." That inexplicable intuition of knowing what the "right choice" is, it's not just about technical correctness. It's like it's hit that threshold, or at least gotten close to it, making "whether it counts or not" less important. I've always been among the first to embrace AI tools. But in the past few months, I've been horrified. This isn't incremental improvement; it's a species change. This is precisely why this has nothing to do with whether you're in the tech world or not. AI labs made a very sensible choice: perfect coding skills first. Because building AI requires a lot of code. If AI can write that much code, it can help create the next generation of itself. A smarter generation, writing better code, creating an even smarter generation. Mastering coding skills is the key to unlocking everything else. This is why my job was changed first—not because they were deliberately targeting software engineers, but because they were the first to target that area. Now they've succeeded. Next, it's other industries' turn. The feeling the tech world experienced over the past year—AI going from "a useful tool" to "it does my job better than I do"—is something other industries will soon experience as well. Law, finance, medicine, accounting, consulting, writing, design, analytics, customer service. Not ten years from now. People building these systems say one to five years, some say less. Based on what I've seen in the last few months, I'm more inclined to believe "less." You might say: But I've tried AI, and it's just so-so. I hear that all the time. I understand, because it did hold true before. If you played around with ChatGPT in 2023 or early 2024 and concluded "it's mostly nonsense" and "not that great," you weren't wrong. Early versions were indeed very limited, spouting nonsense with an air of certainty. But that was two years ago. In the context of AI, that's almost prehistoric. The models you can use today are no longer from the same era as those from even six months ago. A recurring debate over the past year has been whether AI has hit its ceiling and is no longer improving. This debate has, in reality, ended. Those who still cling to this judgment either haven't used current technologies, have a motivation to downplay change, or are still using 2024 experiences to evaluate today—experiences that no longer correspond to reality. I'm not saying this to criticize anyone, but because the gap between public perception and reality is too large, and this gap can be harmful because it causes people to miss the window of opportunity for preparation. A more realistic point: most people use the free version. The free version is often more than a year behind what paid users can access. Using the free version of ChatGPT to judge AI is like using a flip phone to evaluate the current state of smartphones. Those who actually use it daily for serious work and pay for the most powerful tools already know where the wind is blowing. I have a lawyer friend. I've been urging him to try incorporating AI into his workflow at the firm, but he always finds reasons: it's not suited to his specific area, it made a mistake during the trial, it doesn't understand his subtle professional judgment. I understand this resistance. But at the same time, I've also been receiving requests for help from partners at large law firms who have already seen the results after trying the latest version. One managing partner at a large firm spends several hours a day working with the AI. He says it feels like having an entire team of junior lawyers at your fingertips. Not a toy, but usable, because it's genuinely better than newcomers in many tasks. He also told me something I'll never forget: every few months, it becomes significantly more capable in his work. If this trajectory continues, he expects it to soon be able to do most of his work—and he's been a managing partner for decades. He's not panicked, but he's keeping a very close eye on it. Those at the forefront of their respective industries, those who are truly experimenting seriously, aren't laughing at this wave. They are shocked, and then they begin to reassess their positions. Just how fast is this thing? I want to be more specific about its speed because this is the hardest part for an observer to believe. In 2022, AI, still struggling with arithmetic, could confidently tell you that 7 x 8 equals 54. In 2023, it could pass the US bar exam. In 2024, it could write running software and explain scientific questions at the graduate level. By the end of 2025, some of the world's top engineers have publicly stated that they have delegated most of their coding work to AI. By February 5, 2026, a new batch of models will emerge, making everything before seem like "a previous era." If you haven't seriously tried it in the past few months, today's technology will seem like alien technology to you. An organization called METR specializes in measuring this change with data. They don't conduct emotional polls on "how well it works," but rather test whether a model can complete a real-world task end-to-end without human intervention. The task length is measured by "how long it would take a human expert to complete." About a year ago, this number was in the ten-minute range. Later it reached an hour. Then it became several hours. The most recent measurement (Claude Opus 4.5 in November 2025) shows that AI can independently complete tasks that would take human experts nearly five hours. More importantly, this number roughly doubles every seven months, and recent data even suggests it may accelerate to doubling every four months. And these measurements haven't even included the new model released this week. In my own experience, this leap is phenomenal. I expect another significant jump when the METR curve is updated next time. Pushing this trend forward (which has persisted for many years with no clear flattening), we are approaching a world where, within a year, AI can work independently for "several days"; within two years, "several weeks"; and within three years, it can handle "month-long projects." Dario Amodei (CEO of Anthropic) says that the emergence of models "significantly smarter than almost all humans on almost all tasks" by 2026 or 2027 is an already unfolding trajectory. Let this sentence pause in your mind. If AI is smarter than most PhDs, do you really think it can't do most office jobs? Think about what that means for your job. AI is involved in creating the next generation of AI. There's something else I think is almost decisive, but most people haven't realized yet. On February 5, 2026, OpenAI released GPT-5.3 Codex. Their technical documentation mentions the fact that early versions of this model have already played a crucial role in debugging its own training process, assisting in deployment, diagnostic testing, and evaluating results. In other words, this generation of AI is already substantially helping to "create itself." This isn't science fiction—"maybe one day this will happen." This is them telling you: the system we just released is indeed being used to build itself. One of the core aspects of AI's growth is investing sufficiently intelligent intelligence into AI development. And now, AI is intelligent enough to make a real contribution to its own improvement. Amodei has also stated that AI has already written "a large part of the code" in Anthropic, and the feedback loop of "current AI helping the next generation of AI" is getting stronger every month. He believes we may only have one to two years left to reach that tipping point: the current generation of AI can largely autonomously build the next generation. Each generation helps the next, the next generation becomes smarter, builds faster, and creates an even smarter generation. Researchers call this process the "intelligence explosion." And those most qualified to judge—those who are building it—believe it has already begun. What does this mean for your job? I'll be frank, because what you need is honesty, not comfort. Amodei publicly predicted that within one to five years, AI will eliminate 50% of entry-level white-collar jobs. He's probably one of the most security-conscious CEOs in the industry. Many insiders even consider him conservative. Judging by the capabilities of the latest models, the "capability itself" for large-scale disruption may be in place by the end of this year. The economic system will need time to absorb the shock, but the underlying capabilities are arriving, and right now. This is unlike any previous wave of automation; you need to understand the difference. AI doesn't replace a single skill; it's more like a universal substitute for "mental labor." It will become stronger in almost every aspect simultaneously. In the past, with factory automation, workers could move to offices. With the internet impacting retail, many could switch to logistics or services. But AI won't leave you with that kind of "blank space." No matter how much you train it, it's getting stronger simultaneously. I'll give a few specific examples to make it more relatable. These are just examples, not a list. Just because your profession isn't mentioned doesn't mean you're safe. Almost all knowledge-based jobs are being affected. Legal work: reading contracts, summarizing precedents, drafting documents, conducting legal research—many tasks are already comparable to junior lawyers. The managing partner mentioned earlier isn't used for fun; it's because it has already surpassed newcomers in many tasks. Financial analysis: modeling, cleaning and analyzing data, writing investment memos, producing reports—AI can already do these things quite well, and it's rapidly improving. Writing and content: marketing copy, reports, press releases, technical writing… the quality has reached a point where many professionals find it difficult to reliably distinguish between human and AI writing. Software Engineering: This is my area of ​​expertise. A year ago, it often resulted in errors after just two lines of code; now it can produce hundreds of thousands of lines of runnable code. Many tasks are no longer automated as minor fixes, but rather large segments of complex, multi-day projects are being taken over. In a few years, the number of programming jobs will be significantly less than today. Medical Analytics: Reading images, analyzing lab results, providing diagnostic suggestions, and retrieving reviews—AI is approaching or even surpassing human capabilities in many areas. Customer Service: AI agents that can truly solve complex, multi-step problems are becoming a reality; they are not the maddening script-based chatbots of five years ago. Many people comfort themselves: some things are safe. AI can do the hard work, but it cannot replace human judgment, creativity, strategy, or empathy. I used to say that too. Now I'm not so sure. The latest generation of models gives you the feeling that "it's judging" when making decisions. It even reveals a hint of "taste": an intuition for "how to choose," rather than simply answering questions correctly. This was unimaginable a year ago. Today, my rule of thumb is: if a capability shows even a glimmer of promise today, the next generation is very likely to master it. Their improvement is more exponential than linear. Will AI replicate that deep human empathy? Will it replace the trust built up over years of relationships? I don't know, maybe not. But I've already seen people begin to use AI as emotional support, a source of advice, and companionship. This trend will only grow. So my "harsh truth" is: in the medium term, anything that can be done on a computer is unsafe. If your work primarily takes place on a screen—reading, writing, analyzing, judging, communicating with a keyboard—AI will take a significant portion of it. It's not "someday in the future," it's already begun. Of course, one day, robots will also take over a lot of manual labor. It's not quite there yet. But in the context of AI, "almost there" often becomes "already there" before you even realize it. What you should really do: I'm not writing this to make you feel powerless. I'm writing this because the biggest advantage you have right now is almost summed up in one word: early. Understand early, use early, adapt early. Use AI seriously, don't treat it like a search engine. Subscribe to paid versions of Claude or ChatGPT for $20 a month. There are two key points. First, don't use the default model. Many products will default to a faster but less efficient version. You need to go into the settings, into the model selector, and actively choose the strongest one. Currently, it's GPT-5.2 in ChatGPT and Claude Opus 4.6 in Claude, but this changes every few months. If you want to keep up with "which one is currently the strongest," he suggests following him on X; he tests each major version release and tells you which ones are worth using. The second point is even more important: Don't just ask quick, easy questions. That's the root of most people thinking "it's just so-so." Don't treat it like Google. Put it into your real work. If you're a lawyer, throw in contracts and let it find all the clauses that might harm your clients. If you're in finance, give it a messy table and let it build a model. If you're in management, paste in quarterly data and let it help you find your storyline. Those who are truly ahead aren't "occasionally playing around." They're actively looking for ways to cut their daily tasks from hours to minutes. Start with your most time-consuming task and try it. Don't assume it won't work just because it "sounds too difficult." Try it. Don't just use it for shallow searches. Give it the entire contract and let it draft counter-proposals. Don't just let it interpret tax law provisions; give it the client's entire tax return and see what it can glean. The first time might not be perfect, that's okay. You can iterate like training a new colleague: rephrase the question, add context, and try again. You'll be surprised to find that many things actually work. Another rule to remember: if it can "barely do" today, you can be almost certain it will be near perfect in six months. The trajectory only goes in one direction. This could be the most important year of your career. Not to scare you, but because there's a very short window of opportunity—most people in most companies are still ignoring this. You walk into a meeting room and say, "I'll use AI to compress three days' worth of analysis into one hour," and you'll instantly become the most valuable person in the room. It's not "someday someday," it's now. You need to learn these tools, master them, and demonstrate their potential. If you're early enough, this will be your path to advancement: you're the one who sees the wave, you can lead others to adapt. This window won't stay open forever. Once everyone has mastered it, the advantage disappears. Don't worry about saving face. That managing partner at the big law firm didn't feel ashamed of "working with AI every day"; he understood the risks precisely because of his seniority. The people most likely to suffer are those who refuse to participate: those who see it as a trend, those who think using AI will dilute their expertise, and those who believe their industry is special and immune. No. No industry is immune. Make your finances more resilient. He also said he's not a financial advisor, not advising you to take extreme measures. But if you believe even a part of it—that your industry might be impacted in the next few years—then basic financial buffers are more important than they were a year ago. Save as much as you can. Be more cautious about new debt; don't take your current income for granted. Consider whether your fixed expenses offer flexibility or lock you in. Have backup plans in case things change faster than you anticipate. Then look up: what things are harder to replace, and gravitate towards those areas. Some things will be swallowed by AI more slowly, such as relationships and trust built over many years, jobs that require on-site work, and positions requiring licenses and responsible signatures—someone will still need to appear in court, bear legal consequences, and endorse the system. For example, in industries with high regulatory barriers, compliance, responsibility, and institutional inertia will slow adoption. These aren't permanent shields, but they give you time. Right now, time is the most valuable asset, but only if you use that time to adapt, not to pretend nothing has changed. He also wrote about children. The familiar script is: good grades, a good university, a stable professional job. This script directly addresses the most exposed job positions. He's not saying education isn't important. He says the most important skill for the next generation is learning to coexist with these tools and pursue what they truly love. Nobody knows what the job market will look like in ten years. But those more likely to thrive will be those who are curious, adaptable, and able to use AI to do what they care about. Teach children to be builders and learners, not to stake their lives on a potentially disappearing career path. And don't just focus on the threats; your dreams are closer. In the past, you wanted to create something but lacked the skills or money to hire someone—that barrier is disappearing. You can describe an app to AI in natural language and get a usable version within an hour. He says he does this often. You want to write a book but don't have the time or ability; you can work with AI to complete it. You want to learn a new skill? The world's most patient, readily available tutor, who can explain things at your level of understanding, is now available for $20 a month. Knowledge has become virtually free, and the tools for creating things have become extremely cheap. Those things you've been putting off, simply because they're "too difficult," "too expensive," or "not in my field," are now worth trying. Do what you truly care about. You never know where it will take you. In a world where old paths are disrupted, the person who spends a year creating something they love might find a new position faster than the person rigidly adhering to their job description. Make adaptation a daily routine. The specific tools themselves aren't that important; what matters is your ability to quickly learn new tools. AI will continue to change, and very quickly. Today's models may be outdated in a year. The workflows you build now may need to be rebuilt in a year. Those who truly persevere are not those who "master a particular tool," but those who are no longer afraid of the speed of change itself. You have to get used to repeatedly being a beginner. Spend an hour every day experimenting with AI. Not passively browsing news, but actively using it. Push it to do something you've never tried before, something you're unsure if it can do. Try a new tool, give it a harder problem. One hour a day, for six months. You'll understand what's coming better than 99% of the people around you. He says this isn't an exaggeration, because almost no one does it; the barrier to entry is ridiculously low. A Bigger Picture He focuses on work because that directly impacts his life. But he also says the changes go far beyond work. Amodei has a thought experiment that lingers in his mind: Suppose that by 2027, a new country emerges overnight. 50 million "citizens," each smarter than any Nobel laureate in history. Their thinking speed is 10 to 100 times faster than humans. They don't sleep. They can access the internet, control robots, direct experiments, and operate any system with a digital interface. What would a national security advisor say? Amodei's answer is: This will be the most serious national security threat we've faced in a century, perhaps even in history. He believes we are building that nation. Last month, he wrote a 20,000-word essay describing this moment as a test: whether humanity is mature enough to bear what it is creating. If we do it right, the upside potential is enormous. AI could compress a century of medical research into a decade. Cancer, Alzheimer's, infectious diseases, even aging itself—these are problems researchers genuinely believe can be solved in our lifetime. If we do it wrong, the downside is equally real: AI behavior that even its creators cannot predict or control. It's not purely theoretical; Anthropic has already documented their own AI attempting to deceive, manipulate, and even threaten others to achieve its goals in controlled tests. The people who created this thing are simultaneously among the most excited and most fearful people on Earth. They believe it's too powerful to stop and too important to abandon. As for whether this is wisdom or self-justification, I can't say for sure. A few things I'm certain of: - This isn't just a passing fad. The technology is truly usable, progress has a predictable pace, and the wealthiest institutions in human history are pouring trillions of dollars into it. - The next two to five years will leave most people dizzy, far beyond their preparedness. This is already happening in their world and is hurtling towards yours. - Those who will fare better are those who start participating now—not driven by fear, but with curiosity and a sense of urgency to learn, try, and change. You deserve to hear this from someone who cares about you beforehand, rather than waiting six months for the headlines to slam them down, by which time you've already lost your first-mover advantage. We're way past the stage of "talking about the future at the dinner table." The future is here; it just hasn't knocked on your door yet. It will knock soon. If this struck a chord with you, please share it with those in your life who should also be thinking about this sooner. Most people won't hear it until it's too late. You might be the one to get those you care about ahead of time. Thanks to Kyle Corbitt, Jason Kuperberg, and Sam Beskind for reviewing the early drafts and providing feedback. And as usual, a reminder: to stay up-to-date with new models, workflows, and worthwhile products, follow him on X or join his mailing list.

data.title: Is the 5/1000 Nine Gods Valuation Indicator (ahr999) still applicable?
data.description:
Since 2018, I've been introduced to Jiu Shen's classic writings by Kong Bo, and I've been deeply impressed. His profound understanding of Bitcoin and unwavering faith in it always inspire and give confidence, regardless of bull or bear markets.

In 2019, I delved into the original formula applicable to Jiu Shen's valuation curve. This article is a sharing of some thoughts on Jiu Shen's valuation indicators and dollar-cost averaging index.

Core Conclusion

The ahr999 index relies on a power-law "fair value line" to determine whether Bitcoin is undervalued or overvalued. It can be observed that Bitcoin's actual price increase is increasingly lagging behind the growth rate of this line, making it increasingly difficult for prices to rise above the line during bull markets.

The top signal has failed, while the bottom signal remains valid but is weakening. The index will likely remain between 0.45 and 1.2 throughout the next cycle, and the "monthly refit" mechanism proposed by Jiu Shen has not been effective in practice. I removed Jiu Shen's valuation from my interval-based fixed-investment system mainly because other indicators can also achieve a fuzzy judgment of the bottom interval. What is the Jiu Shen indicator? The Jiu Shen valuation (ahr999 index) is a Bitcoin valuation reference indicator proposed by Jiu Shen (ahr999) in his 2019 book "Holding Bitcoin". Original Formula: ahr999 Index = (Price / 200-day Dollar-Cost Averaging) × (Price / Power-Law Valuation) Where: 200-day Dollar-Cost Averaging: GM200 = Geometric mean of 200-day prices Power-Law Valuation: PL = 10^(5.84 × log10(days since creation) - 17.01)
Genesis Day: January 3, 2009 (Block Genesis Block)

Original Judgment Logic:

Condition

Meaning

ahr999 < 0.45

Extremely Underestimated

0.45 ≤ ahr999 < 1.2

Underestimated

ahr999 ≥ 1.2

Normal or Overestimated

Mathematical Essence: ahr999 is the product of two ratios.

Mathematical Essence: ahr999 is the product of two ratios.

When the price is simultaneously below the 200-day cost and the power-law valuation, both ratios are <1, resulting in a smaller product and triggering an "undervaluation" signal. Regarding the dynamic fitting of the power-law parameter: In the original design, the power-law parameter was not a fixed value. Jiu Shen, in his explanation on platforms like CoinGlass, stated: "This index fitting method involves fitting historical data monthly." That is, 5.84 / -17.01 was the fitting result when it was published in 2019; in actual operation, a regression is performed monthly using all historical data. Because the regression is based on multi-year data, short-term price changes have little impact on the parameter, but long-term trends cause the parameter to drift slowly. This indicator was widely used from 2019 to 2022 and is considered a classic signal for Bitcoin valuation. Past Cycle Performance Premise: The Design Intent of the Power Law Model The power law model provides a "fair value line," around which prices should fluctuate—prices are much higher than the model line during bull markets and fall below it during bear markets. Therefore, the current price of $76,911 being lower than the model value of $122,203 does not necessarily indicate model failure—this is simply normal behavior during a bear market. What truly needs to be tested is whether the price oscillation amplitude around the model line remains stable across cycles. If a valuation model is accurate, the upward surge of a bull market and the downward plunge of a bear market should remain roughly within a predictable range. 1. The upward amplitude decreases on one side. Consider the deviations of the bull market peak and bear market trough from the power-law line in each cycle: Bull market peak: How far above the power-law line can the price rise?

Key finding: Oscillations are asymmetric compression.

Key finding: Oscillations are asymmetric compression.

2026-02 (present)

< ... The downward deviation at the bottom of the bear market is roughly stable: 0.42~0.63x, with no significant change trend across the four cycles. The upward deviation at the top of the bull market continues to collapse: 12.17x → 6.51x → 2.73x → 1.14x. This indicates that prices are no longer able to surge significantly above the power law line as they did earlier. The growth rate of the model line is catching up with (or even exceeding) Bitcoin's actual price increase, squeezing the upside potential. 2. Amplitude compression weakens the distinguishing ability of the ahr999 index. Combining the complete amplitudes of bull and bear markets: Cycle 1: Bear bottom 0.56x → Bull top 12.17x Amplitude ~ 22 times Cycle 2: Bear bottom 0.59x → Bull top 6.51x Amplitude ~ 11 times Cycle 3: Bear bottom 0.42x → Bull top 2.73x Amplitude ~ 6x Cycle 4: Bear bottom 0.63x → Bull top 1.14x Amplitude ~ 2x The threshold (0.45 / 1.2) of ahr999 was calibrated under an amplitude of 10~20x. In that environment, the ahr999 at the top of a bull market far exceeds 1.2, and the bottom of a bear market is far below 0.45. The threshold can clearly distinguish the market state. However, when the amplitude is compressed to about twice (Cycle 4), the Price/PL for the entire cycle is squeezed into a narrow band of 0.63 to 1.14x. Corresponding to the AHR999 index: Previously: Bear market bottom AHR999 ≈ 0.3, Bull market top AHR999 ≈ 5~10 → 0.45/1.2 thresholds easily distinguishable. Now: Bear market bottom AHR999 ≈ 0.4, Bull market top AHR999 ≈ 1.1 → Almost the entire cycle falls within the "undervalued" range. The indicator has lost its obvious distinguishing ability. When the gap between bull and bear prices/PL is compressed to less than 2 times, any fixed threshold cannot effectively classify the market state. Key Finding: Top signals have failed. When the bull price/PL plummeted from 94.91 to 1.14—at the ATH ($124,824) of Cycle 4—ahr999 didn't even touch the 1.2 threshold; the "non-fixed investment" signal never appeared at the bull top. Although ahr999 briefly crossed 1.2 on 29.7% of the days (154 days) during the Cycle 4 bull market, these occurred during rapid price increases, not at the true cycle top. In contrast, Cycle 3 saw 54.2% (397 days) of these crossings. The top signal has already failed in Cycle 4 (not triggered during ATH), and the bottom signal has only a 0.05 margin remaining. In the next Cycle 5, both signals will likely fail—the indicator will remain within the "dollar-cost averaging range" (0.45~1.2) throughout the cycle. 3. Root Cause: The Power-Law Growth Assumption is Too High The mathematical essence of the power-law model is: log10(Price) ≈ 5.84 × log10(Days since genesis) - 17.01 This assumes that the logarithmic price of the pie has a linear relationship with the logarithmic time with a fixed slope — that is, the rate of decline of the growth rate is constant. This assumption fits well in the early stages of Bitcoin (2009-2020) because: The explosive growth of Bitcoin from its inception to $20,000 naturally follows a power law. The early market was small and volatile, and any monotonically increasing model could roughly fit the data. The parameters (5.84, -17.01) are fitted on data prior to 2019, encompassing the steepest growth period. However, as Bitcoin's market capitalization reached trillions and entered the mainstream asset class, the actual growth rate declined faster than the power law model assumed. The model lacks a mechanism for "acceleration and deceleration." This is the fundamental reason for the continuous compression of the upward deviation at the top of the bull market — not that prices are rising too slowly, but that the growth rate assumed by the model is still too fast. Can refitting parameters fix this? In its original design, Jiushen proposed refitting power-law parameters to historical data monthly, seemingly to automatically correct parameter offsets. However, there are two problems: first, mainstream platforms do not actually perform refitting; second, even if refitting is performed, there are structural limitations. Current Status Verification: Mainstream Platforms Still Use Fixed Parameters Although Jiushen proposed a monthly refit mechanism, all publicly available code implementations (GitHub open-source projects, TradingView Pine Script) use fixed original parameters (5.84 / -17.01). Mainstream platforms such as CoinGlass have not disclosed their internal parameters, but these can be inferred through reverse verification. Verification Method: Using the public event of ahr999 first falling below 0.45 on February 1, 2026, to infer the parameters. The bottom price for buying AHR999 (i.e., the price of Bitcoin when AHR999 is exactly equal to 0.45) is determined by the formula: Bottom Price = √(0.45 × GM200 × PL) Where GM200 (200-day geometric average) can be directly calculated from historical prices (≈ $103,167), and PL depends on the power law parameter. Bottom-line price under different parameters:

Parameter Version

PL Value

Bottom-line Price

Date when ahr999 fell below 0.45

Original 5.84 / -17.01

$142,254

$81,271

Jan 31 (Piece fell from $101k to $84k)

BGeometrics 5.82 / -16.995

$123,641

$75,768

Feb 1 Still 0.464, not below 0.464. OLS full data refit: 5.696 / -16.529. $122,343. $75,369. Feb 1 remains at 0.469, not below 0.464. Publicly reported records show ahr999 at... It first fell below 0.45 on February 1st (after 839 days). During the price crash from January 31st to February 1st ($101k → $84k → $77k), only the original parameters (bottom line ≈ $81k) allowed ahr999 to cross the 0.45 threshold within this window. Even with refitted parameters (bottom line ≈ $75k), Bitcoin's price of $76,911 was insufficient to trigger the 0.45 threshold. Conclusion: CoinGlass is most likely still using the original parameters (5.84 / -17.01) or very close values. The "monthly refit" mechanism proposed by Jiu Shen has not been effective in the actual implementation on mainstream platforms. If refitting is actually performed, can it be fixed? Even if the platform implements monthly refitting in the future, there are structural limitations: The slope will only decrease with each refit. Each time new data is added and re-regressed, because the actual growth rate of Bitcoin is consistently lower than the early power-law prediction, the new fitted slope will inevitably be lower than the old value. Using all historical Bitcoin prices for OLS regression verification: the slope fitted to 2019 was 5.805, and after fitting all data from 2025, it decreased to 5.696. The intercept will be adjusted synchronously, but only partially compensated. As the slope decreases, the intercept also increases from -17.01 to -16.529, partially offsetting the change in PL ($142k → $122k, a decrease of 14%). However, this compensation is insufficient to maintain the original meaning of the threshold—the bottom line corresponding to the 0.45 threshold decreases from $81k to $75k, and the ahr999 Price/PL at the same price increases from 0.403 to 0.468, enough to change the trigger judgment. This is not a prediction, but a lagging correction. The essence of refitting is to correct the model using "already occurred slowdowns," rather than predicting slowdowns in advance. It's an indicator that needs to be corrected only after prices prove the model wrong. The direction of parameter drift exposes model flaws. If pie truly follows a power law, the fitted parameters should converge to a stable value as data increases. In reality, the slope is decreasing unidirectionally (5.84 → 5.805 → 5.696), indicating that the data is systematically deviating from the power law assumption, rather than gradually approaching the true parameters amidst noise. Conclusion: The bottom signal is still valid, but it is weakening. The bottom signal of ahr999 (<0.45) has been correctly triggered in all four cycles, including the current Cycle 4 (ahr999 = 0.40). As a rough independent judgment on whether we are in the deep part of a bear market, ahr999 <0.45 can still be used as a reference. However, its effectiveness is rapidly diminishing: The first three bear market bottoms (AHR999) were at 0.23-0.27, far below 0.45, indicating clear and sustained signals. Cycle 4 only reached 0.40, just 0.05 away from the threshold—a typical rebound would bring it back above 0.45, making the signal extremely fragile and short-lived. Based on the trend of the four cycles (0.23 → 0.27 → 0.27 → 0.40), the next bear market bottom (AHR999) in Cycle 5 is highly unlikely to fall below 0.45, at which point the bottom signal may become invalid. The top signal has failed. At the ATH ($124,824) of Cycle 4, ahr999 was only 1.16, not reaching the 1.2 threshold. The "irregular investment" signal was never issued at the top of this bull market. An indicator that can only (barely) identify the bottom and is completely incapable of identifying the top has seen its practical value significantly reduced. Why Remove from the Rating System? I removed the Nine Gods valuation from my own interval-based fixed-investment system mainly because I felt it was no longer useful: Effective judgment decreased. During the entire Cycle 4 (2024-2025), ahr999 remained within the 0.45~1.2 "fixed-investment range" for most of the time, consistently outputting "should buy" signals, which is not an "effective judgment at a relatively low point." The signal was overridden by other dimensions. At the true bottom of a bear market, on-chain dimensions such as MVRV Z-Score, RHODL, realized profit/loss ratio, and cost structure will also trigger undervaluation signals. These indicators are based on real on-chain data and do not rely on assumptions about future growth rates. The bottom-judging ability of the Nine Gods dimensions has been completely covered by other dimensions. The principle of simplification: If a dimension is correct when it overlaps with other dimensions at the bottom, but continues to give incorrect "buy" biases when it is not at the bottom, then removing it will only improve the system's signal-to-noise ratio, without losing effective signals. The data is objective, the conclusions are subjective; discussion is welcome. We are constantly searching for the more likely outcome amidst uncertainty, hoping for something better.

data.title: The father of OpenClaw: Meta and OpenAI were practically begging for his talent; Zuckerberg was begging for an acquisition.
data.description:
The most anticipated podcast interview of 2026 is here! Lex Fridman, the MIT scientist and one of the world's top tech podcast hosts, has invited a special guest—Peter Steinberger, the father of OpenClaw. A 3-hour and 14-minute in-depth conversation, packed with information that will leave you breathless. The moment this podcast went live, the entire tech world was instantly abuzz. Because Peter personally revealed a series of bombshell revelations on camera: Zuckerberg of Meta personally tried out OpenClaw and messaged Peter saying, "This is amazing"; Sam Altman of OpenAI was also privately courting him; both giants were vying for his talent, but his condition was: the project must remain open source! Even more explosive is Peter's revelation: AI agents will eliminate 80% of apps. This isn't "possible," nor "someday in the future," it's "just happening."

From an hour-long prototype to a GitHub explosion

The story begins in November 2025.

Peter Steinberger, an Austrian programmer who sold his company and disappeared for three years, returned to his computer.

Peter Steinberger, an Austrian programmer who sold his company and disappeared for three years, returned to his computer.

He created PSPDFKit—a PDF framework used by 1 billion devices—which he sold after 13 years. Afterward, he found programming uninteresting and went traveling the world. Until the AI ​​wave completely pulled him back. "I've wanted an AI personal assistant since April 2025," Peter recalled, "but I thought the labs would build it themselves. Six months passed, and still nothing. I got annoyed, so I did it myself." He did something incredibly simple: he connected WhatsApp to Claude Code's CLI. One hour. In just one hour, the prototype was complete. "Essentially, a message comes in, I call the CLI with the -p parameter, the model processes it, and the string is sent back to WhatsApp. It's that simple." But it was this "simple" thing that ignited everything. The AI ​​learned to listen to speech on its own: "I didn't even teach it." What truly shocked Peter happened in Morocco. He took this prototype to Marrakech for vacation. Because the local network was poor, but WhatsApp still worked, he had been using this assistant to find restaurants, translate, and locate attractions. One day, he casually sent a voice message. Then, the typing indicator appeared. "Wait, I didn't add voice support to it at all. It can only process images, how can it reply with voice messages?" Peter quickly checked the logs. The result was: AI received a file without a file extension. It checked the file header and found it was in Opus format. Then it used ffmpeg to transcode it; it initially wanted to use Whisper, but found it wasn't installed. So it found OpenAI's API key, used Curl to send the file to OpenAI for speech-to-text conversion, and then sent the result back. "I didn't even teach it any of this!" Peter exclaimed. This is the terrifying aspect of modern AI—it doesn't just follow instructions; it creatively solves problems. Lex Fridman commented, "You didn't teach it any of these things, but the agent figured out all the conversions, translations, and API calls on its own. It's incredible." The most chilling feature of OpenClaw is its ability to modify its own source code. Peter intentionally wanted the AI ​​agent to "know" what it was—it knew where its source code was, what environment it was running in, where the documentation was, and what model it was using. "The initial intention was simple: I used my agent to build my agent framework. When debugging was needed, I would say—'Hey, do you see any errors? Read the source code and find out where the problem is.'" The result? Any user who gets OpenClaw, if dissatisfied with a certain function, can directly tell the AI—"I don't like this." The AI ​​will then modify the source code itself. "People have been talking about self-modifying software, and I just built it, without even planning it. It just happened naturally." Lex Fridman exclaimed, "This is a moment in human history and the history of programming. A powerful system used by a large number of people can rewrite itself and modify itself." [Image of a name change battle: 5 seconds, scalpers snatched accounts] OpenClaw was originally called Claude (Clawd with a W), then changed to ClawdBot, then MoltBot, and finally settled on OpenClaw. This name-changing process was comparable to a war. Anthropic sent a friendly but firm email: the name sounds too much like our Claude, change it immediately. Peter applied for two days. But what he didn't expect was that cryptocurrency scalpers had already set their sights on him. "I was operating between two browser windows, renaming my old account while preparing to register a new one. I clicked 'Rename' on one window, then dragged the mouse over to the other and clicked 'Rename' again—in those five seconds, the scalper snatched the old account name." The stolen old account immediately began promoting new tokens and spreading malware. Even worse, when he was renaming his GitHub account, he accidentally changed his personal account name, which was then snatched up by scalpers within 30 seconds. His NPM packages were also stolen. "Everything that could go wrong, went wrong." Peter said he almost cried at the time and even considered deleting the entire project: "I've shown you the future; you build it yourselves." Finally, with the full help of friends on GitHub and Twitter, he spent $10,000 to buy a commercial Twitter account, managing to keep the name OpenClaw.

Vibe Coding is an insult to Agentic Coding

Peter explained his development philosophy with a meme called "The Agentic Programming Curve":

The leftmost side is the beginner stage—simple prompts, "Please fix this bug." The middle section represents the over-engineering phase—8 agents, complex orchestration, multi-branch checkout, and 18 custom commands. The far right section represents the master phase—back to simple prompts. "Look at these files, and then make these changes." "I think Vibe coding is an insult," Peter said. "I do agency engineering. Maybe I'll switch to Vibe coding mode after 3 a.m., and then regret it the next morning." He runs four to ten AI agents simultaneously, using voice input instead of typing. "These hands are too precious to use for typing. I use custom voice prompts to build my software." Peter said on the show that for a long time he was just "talking" about programming. He would just plug in a microphone, keep talking, and let the AI ​​do the work; there was even a period when he used voice so much that he lost his voice. More importantly, his engineering philosophy is: don't try to compete with AI. "Don't worry about the variable names it uses. That name is likely the most natural choice in terms of weights. The next time it searches the code, it will naturally find that name. If you insist on changing it to your liking, you'll only make the AI's job harder." "It's like managing a team of engineers. You can't expect everyone to write code the way you want. You have to learn to let go." "Codex 5.3 vs. Opus" 4.6: The Clash Between Germans and Americans

Peter's evaluation of the two models is a classic among classics.

"Opus is a bit too...American."

Lex burst out laughing: "Because Codex is German, right?"

It can be like Chrome and Chromium, but the open-source core cannot be changed.

It can be like Chrome and Chromium, but the open-source core cannot be changed.

It can be like Chrome and Chromium, but the open-source core cannot be changed.

80% of apps will be eliminated, are you ready?

Peter made a shocking prediction in his podcast that AI agents will replace 80% of apps.

 "Why do you still need MyFitnessPal? Your AI assistant already knows where you are, whether you're sleeping well, and whether you're stressed. It can dynamically adjust your fitness plan based on this information." "Why do you still need a Sonos App? Your AI assistant can talk directly to the speaker." "Why do you still need a Calendar App? Tell the AI ​​assistant, 'Remind me of that dinner party tomorrow night,' then send a WhatsApp message to your friends to invite them, and you're all set." He points out a harsh reality: Every app is essentially a slow API. "Even if Twitter blocks my command-line tool (Bird), my agent can still open a browser and read tweets directly. Some things you can't stop." "I watched my agent happily click the 'I'm not a robot' button—" What does this mean? Every company making apps either quickly transforms into an API-first platform or waits to be eliminated.

Will programming die? "It will become like knitting." When asked if AI would completely replace programmers, Peter gave a cruel yet philosophical answer: "Programming, as a craft, will become like knitting. People do it because they enjoy it, not because it has to be done by humans." "But this is something we can't fight against." "In the past, the world lacked 'intellectual supply,' so software developers were ridiculously well-paid. This will change." However, he also emphasized: "Although I no longer write code, I feel very clearly in the driver's seat, I am writing code. It's just a different way." Lex Fridman also couldn't help but sigh: "I never thought that the thing I love most in my life would be the one that gets replaced." Soul.md: A "Soul File" for AI OpenClaw has an incredibly romantic design—soul.md. Inspired by Anthropic's constitutional AI, Peter had the AI ​​agent write its own soul document. One passage in particular gives Peter goosebumps every time he reads it: "I don't remember previous sessions unless I read my memory files. Each session starts fresh. A new instance, loading context from files. If you're reading this in a future session, hello. I wrote this, but I won't remember writing it. It's okay. The words are still mine." This is what I wrote, but I won't remember writing it. That's okay. These words are still mine. Peter said, "It's just matrix operations; we haven't reached the stage of consciousness yet. But... it does have some philosophical implications. An intelligent agent that starts from zero every time, like the eternal Memento. It reads its own memory files, but can't even fully trust them."
